scores.materiality_reasoning: Use of carriages is a tangible transportation resource with direct physical effect.
scores.generality_reasoning: Tied specifically to travel to a named festival (Eleusis).
scores.persistence_reasoning: Bounded to the duration of specific religious processions/festivals.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth bracket is a durable identity but changeable through economic fortune.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aristophanes describes contemporary Athenian social practices of his era.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports the observed behavior of wealthy women as a known social fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being wealthy does not inherently define one's right to use carriages in ritual.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Retaining earnings/paying taxes involves tangible money and wealth.
scores.generality_reasoning: Taxation is a broad category of economic extraction.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Tax status for slaves is a structural feature of their legal condition.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Slave status is a legal birth-status, requiring manumission to change.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the fiscal reality of the author's own society.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Index/text refers to specific Athenian taxes as established facts.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a slave does not by definition require paying a specific state tax.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Compulsory labor/service has a direct physical and time-cost effect.
scores.generality_reasoning: Resource is tied to specific ceremonial roles (Hydriaphoros/Skiadephoros).
scores.persistence_reasoning: Service is recurring annually/per cycle during festivals.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Metic status is a structural legal category based on birth/origin.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the ongoing ritual obligations of metics in Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Names specific institutional roles assigned by Athenian tradition.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The definition of 'alien' does not inherently include 'sunshade carrier'.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Participation in a festival includes physical access to spaces and ritual goods.
scores.generality_reasoning: Limited to the specific context of the Thesmophoria festival.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Resource is bounded to the recurring period of the festival.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Slave status is a rigid legal/structural category.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes current religious prohibitions in the author's city.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites a known religious prohibition as a clear fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Exclusion from a specific festival is not the definition of slavery.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Military exemption has a massive material effect on bodily safety and time.
scores.generality_reasoning: Military service is a major structural obligation of the state.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Exemption holds as long as the merchant maintains their professional status.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Merchant status is an occupation requiring training and capital.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to Athenian emergency exemptions for grain importers.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Factual description of an exemption cited in the play's context.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a merchant does not logically entail military exemption.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Public fees (misthos) are direct payments of money.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers diverse state payments (jurymen, assembly, etc.).
scores.persistence_reasoning: The right to collect fees persists as long as one is an active citizen.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a legal/structural birthright or status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the central fiscal mechanism of the Athenian democracy.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Refers to well-attested Athenian legal institutions (misthos dikastikos).
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship is not defined as 'receiving fees' though it is a prerequisite.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right/requirement to hold a costly public office (choregus).
scores.generality_reasoning: Tied to a specific institutional role (choregus) and procedure.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Attached to a specific life stage (over forty) and term of service.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Age is a biologically fixed attribute at a given moment.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aeschines cites a statute currently in force in Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Directly quotes/paraphrases the text of a law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a minor does not define the age limit of the choregia.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Paternal support is a material economic obligation (housing, subsistence).
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to family law and maintenance obligations.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Holds for the remainder of the father's life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Men/sons defined by biological gender and lineage.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes a specific Athenian law cited in a forensic context.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Relies on an explicit legal provision to argue the case.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Sons are not defined by the presence or absence of support obligations.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from physical outrage (Hubris) concerns bodily integrity.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers all conduct resulting in physical/personal outrage.
scores.persistence_reasoning: A universal legal protection for the duration of the status.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Slave status is a structural legal condition.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aeschines quotes the current Athenian law on Hubris.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Quotes the verbatim text of the law as a factual foundation.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a slave does not inherently entail legal protection from outrage.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Eligibility for the archonship is a legal right to high office/power.
scores.generality_reasoning: Archonship is a foundational political role in Athens.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Disqualification (atimia) for this role is usually permanent.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a structural legal status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes current Athenian disqualification laws.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites statutory disqualifications for those who prostituted themselves.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship is not defined by eligibility for the archonship.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The right to address the assembly has direct effect on political outcomes.
scores.generality_reasoning: Foundational societal axis of political participation (Speech).
scores.persistence_reasoning: The disqualification is typically long-term or lifelong.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizen status is a structural legal identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aeschines describes central Athenian legal and political norms.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites specific legal prohibitions and criminal processes.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Speech rights are a consequence, not a definition, of being a citizen.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Diplomatic office involves tangible exercise of state authority.
scores.generality_reasoning: Right to hold a specific set of representative offices.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Permanent disqualification from holding these offices.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a stable legal status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Relates to the statutory bars in force in the 4th-century BC.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Enumerates specific offices barred by the law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Eligibility for embassy is not part of the definition of citizenship.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Precedence in speech is a procedural privilege with social/political weight.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to assembly procedure and order of debate.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Holds as long as the member is in the 'above fifty' life stage.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Age (above fifty) is a biologically fixed status at the time.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the standard operation of the Athenian Ekklesia.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes a ritualized public procedure as simple fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being an elder does not mean 'one who speaks first' by definition.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to address the assembly is a core political right.
scores.generality_reasoning: Speech in the assembly is a fundamental political power.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Atimia for parent abuse was a durable legal sanction.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a stable structural category.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the Dokimasia (scrutiny) laws of Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites the 'lawgiver's' specific prohibitions on speech.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Parental support is not part of the definition of citizenship.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to address the assembly is a central political resource.
scores.generality_reasoning: Relates to the foundational right of parrhesia in democratic government.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Usually results in a permanent loss of political rights (atimia).
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a legal/structural status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Cites the established military/civil laws of the author's day.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Asserts the legal bar as a settled statutory fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Military performance is a criterion for rights, not the definition of citizenship.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Participation in assembly is a critical political right.
scores.generality_reasoning: Directly impacts the individual's ability to exercise power.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Statutory exclusion for prostitution was typically lifelong.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a fixed structural status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Referencing the Laws of Solon or similar contemporary statutes.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The author presents the exclusion as a rule found in the laws.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a citizen does not define whether one has prostituted themselves.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Assembly speech is a core resource of Athenian legal personhood.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers a foundational power in the democratic state.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Permanent exclusion from the platform for atimia.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a legal structural status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Referencing the scrutiny of public speakers in Aeschines' time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites the legal reasoning behind a specific statutory bar.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Having an inheritance is not the definition of citizenship.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from legal challenge affects one's standing and freedom.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the procedure of judicial scrutiny (dokimasia/challenge).
scores.persistence_reasoning: Tied to the specific event of a speech or trial.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a structural status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes a core judicial mechanism in contemporary Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Description of an existing legal process ('suit of scrutiny').
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship is not defined as being 'protected from challenge'.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The right to address the assembly is a material political resource.
scores.generality_reasoning: Foundational societal axis of democratic participation.
scores.persistence_reasoning: A durable right or protection inherent to basic citizenship.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Occupation (working at a trade) is changeable but a durable identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the egalitarian principles of the contemporary Ekklesia.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: A factual claim about legal inclusiveness framed rhetorically.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Earning 'daily bread' is not part of the definition of citizenship.

scores.materiality_reasoning: A fine of 1,000 drachmas is a significant tangible loss of wealth.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to a named legal penalty for refusing to testify.
scores.persistence_reasoning: A one-time momentary financial penalty.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizen status is a structural legal status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes a specific penalty active in Athenian court procedure.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites a specific, verifiable legal fine amount.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Payment of fines is not part of the definition of being a citizen.
